proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies denoising as a separate step or requires clean flow for training, our method's core novelty is the joint, synergistic learning of the shape subspace and the denoising function directly from the noisy alignment task itself.

proposals.title: Neural Scene Flow Priors for Monocular 3D Face Alignment from Refined 2D Tracks
proposals.motivation: Monocular 3D face reconstruction is crucial for AR/VR and facial animation but remains ill-posed due to depth ambiguity. While recent methods like Deformable NeRF and Neural Scene Flow have shown success in modeling dynamic 3D scenes, they are not directly optimized for the specific, constrained domain of human faces. Leveraging high-fidelity 2D facial tracks, which are increasingly accurate, offers a strong but underutilized signal for 3D shape recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a neural scene flow prior, trained on facial motion sequences, into a coordinate network architecture will enable robust and temporally consistent 3D face alignment from monocular 2D trajectory inputs, outperforming frame-by-frame optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a coordinate network (e.g., an MLP with sinusoidal activations) that maps a canonical 3D face coordinate and a time parameter to a deformed 3D position and scene flow vector. The network will be trained using a multi-term loss: 1) a 2D reprojection loss against refined input tracks, 2) a scene flow consistency loss (inspired by 'Fast Neural Scene Flow') to enforce temporal coherence, and 3) a curvature-aware regularization term (from 'Curvature-Aware Training') to promote smooth and plausible facial deformations. Training will use a dataset of multi-view facial performances to provide weak 3D supervision for the flow field.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for monocular 3D face alignment that explicitly models temporal dynamics via neural scene flow, leading to more stable and accurate reconstructions. It will demonstrate how scene flow and coordinate network methods, typically applied to general scenes, can be specialized and improved for a structured domain like faces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a neural scene flow prior as a core component for monocular 3D face alignment, moving beyond static or independent-frame models. This approach uniquely combines temporal flow supervision, coordinate network efficiency, and domain-specific regularization within a single end-to-end trainable system.

proposals.title: Neural Scene Flow Priors as Convolutional Dictionaries for End-to-End Supervised Tasks
proposals.motivation: Recent work on neural scene flow, such as Fast Neural Scene Flow, demonstrates that coordinate networks can learn powerful, compact representations of dynamic 3D scenes. However, these representations are typically used for reconstruction or flow estimation alone. Leveraging the learned internal features of such networks as generic, task-agnostic priors for downstream supervised learning remains unexplored, potentially offering a new paradigm for data-efficient learning in vision tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the convolutional filter dictionaries learned by coordinate networks for scene flow estimation capture fundamental, transferable priors about 3D structure and motion, and that these features can serve as a highly effective initialization or fixed feature extractor for end-to-end supervised classification and regression models.
proposals.method: We will first train a coordinate-based neural network (e.g., a modified version of Fast Neural Scene Flow) on large-scale dynamic scene datasets. We will then extract its early-layer convolutional filters as a fixed dictionary. This dictionary will be integrated into a lightweight task-specific network head (e.g., a multi-layer perceptron or a small CNN) for supervised tasks like dynamic scene classification or action recognition. The entire pipeline will be trained end-to-end, with the dictionary weights either frozen or fine-tuned, and we will benchmark performance against standard ImageNet-initialized features and randomly initialized networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel method for transferring geometric and dynamic priors from neural scene representations to supervised tasks, potentially improving data efficiency and generalization. It will also provide new insights into the representational content learned by coordinate networks, bridging the gap between neural scene representation and traditional supervised computer vision.
proposals.novelty: While using pre-trained features is common, utilizing features from a coordinate network trained for an unsupervised 3D scene flow task as a convolutional dictionary for 2D/3D supervised learning is a novel cross-domain transfer. This moves beyond using these networks purely for reconstruction, repurposing their internal representations as foundational visual priors.

proposals.title: Learning to Segment Motion: Integrating Robust Flow Priors into Neural Scene Flow Estimation
proposals.motivation: Current neural scene flow and dynamic NeRF methods often rely on pre-computed optical flow from classical algorithms (e.g., RAFT) for supervision or motion segmentation. These fixed flows are brittle under occlusions, fast motion, and textureless regions, introducing errors that propagate into the 3D reconstruction and flow estimation. A more robust, context-aware flow representation is needed to improve the accuracy and generalization of neural scene understanding in complex, real-world sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learned optical flow estimator, jointly optimized with the scene flow network, will provide a more adaptive and robust motion prior, leading to significant improvements in 3D scene flow accuracy and the quality of reconstructed dynamic geometry.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture where a lightweight, learnable flow network (building on RAFT's iterative correlation updates) processes image pairs in parallel with a coordinate-based scene flow network. Instead of a fixed flow loss, we will employ a self-supervised consistency loss between the learned 2D flow and the projected 3D scene flow, regularized by curvature-aware smoothness terms. The flow estimator's weights will be updated end-to-end alongside the scene flow parameters, allowing it to adapt to the specific scene and noise characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified framework for learning scene-specific optical flow priors for dynamic 3D vision. It will demonstrate measurable gains in scene flow accuracy on benchmarks like FlyingThings3D and real-world captures. The method will also provide insights into the co-design of 2D and 3D motion representations within coordinate networks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed flow for supervision, our key novelty is the end-to-end joint optimization of a neural flow estimator within a coordinate network framework for dynamic scenes, creating a synergistic, adaptive motion representation rather than a static supervisory signal.

proposals.title: Dynamic Scene Priors for Monocular Neural Scene Flow and Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Monocular dynamic scene reconstruction methods, like Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) for video, often struggle with complex, unconstrained motion and accurate segmentation of dynamic objects from the background. Pre-2026, methods like D-NeRF and NSFF handle simple motion but lack robustness in real-world videos with multiple, independently moving objects, leading to artifacts and poor scene flow estimation. Improving motion segmentation within the coordinate network framework is crucial for applications in robotics, augmented reality, and 3D content creation from casual video.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learnable, latent motion segmentation field into a coordinate-based neural scene representation will enable more accurate decomposition of complex dynamic scenes from monocular video, improving both reconstruction quality and scene flow estimation without explicit 2D optical flow supervision.
proposals.method: We will extend a deformable NeRF architecture by adding a lightweight, coordinate-based network branch that outputs a per-point motion affinity vector. This branch will be trained jointly with the scene representation using a combination of photometric reconstruction loss, a scene flow smoothness loss (inspired by Fast Neural Scene Flow), and a novel entropy-based sparsity loss on the motion affinity field to encourage discrete segmentation. The curvature-aware training techniques from Lucey's prior work will be applied to stabilize the optimization of this new, under-constrained segmentation field.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for monocular dynamic scene understanding that jointly reasons about geometry, appearance, motion, and object identity. It will provide a new benchmark for dynamic object segmentation within neural volumetric video and deliver more robust and accurate scene flow estimates compared to flow-supervised methods that rely on potentially noisy 2D cues.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, gradient-based learning of a 3D motion segmentation field from video without pre-computed optical flow or instance masks, and the integration of this field's inductive biases to regularize the challenging problem of decomposing complex scene dynamics in a coordinate network.

proposals.title: Semantic Priors for Robust Neural Scene Flow and Depth
proposals.motivation: Neural scene flow and depth estimation from monocular video are fundamentally ambiguous, especially in textureless regions or under complex motion. Pre-2026 methods like Deformable NeRF and coordinate networks struggle with these ambiguities, leading to noisy geometry and incorrect motion segmentation. Semantic segmentation, which provides strong object-level priors, is an underutilized cue that could resolve these ambiguities by enforcing consistency within semantically coherent regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating semantic segmentation priors into the training of coordinate-based neural scene representations will provide strong geometric and motion constraints, leading to more accurate, physically plausible depth and scene flow estimates without requiring additional supervised labels.
proposals.method: We will extend a coordinate network architecture (e.g., an MLP mapping 4D spacetime coordinates to color and scene flow) by adding a lightweight semantic head. This network will be trained jointly on multi-view video with photometric, flow, and semantic losses. The key innovation is a semantic consistency loss: we will regularize predicted depth and flow fields such that their gradients are aligned with boundaries from a pre-trained, frozen 2D semantic segmentation network (e.g., DeepLabV3+), encouraging smoothness within objects and discontinuities at their borders. We will also explore using semantic labels to weight the contribution of flow supervision, down-weighting potentially unreliable flow in dynamic object regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new training framework that leverages 2D semantic knowledge to improve 3D scene understanding, specifically for dynamic neural radiance fields. It will demonstrate quantifiable improvements in depth and scene flow accuracy on benchmarks like KITTI and Dynamic Scene Datasets. The method will provide a principled way to integrate high-level vision priors into coordinate-based neural representations.
proposals.novelty: While semantic segmentation has been used in traditional SLAM, its integration as a soft, training-time prior for regularizing neural implicit scene flow and depth fields is novel. This approach differs from post-hoc fusion or using semantics as input, instead using it as a structural guide for the coordinate network's gradient field during optimization.

proposals.title: Adversarial Depth Prior: GAN-Based Realism for Neural Scene Flow and Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Neural radiance fields (NeRF) and scene flow methods often predict depth as an intermediate product. While supervised losses like L1 or L2 enforce per-pixel accuracy, they fail to capture the complex, holistic statistical properties of real-world depth distributions, leading to artifacts like over-smoothing, unrealistic discontinuities, and poor generalization in unseen views. This limits the realism and utility of 3D reconstructions for downstream tasks like robotics and augmented reality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an adversarial training framework, where a discriminator network is trained to distinguish predicted depth maps from ground-truth depth distributions, will force the coordinate-based neural scene representation to generate depth maps with more realistic statistical properties, thereby improving overall reconstruction quality and robustness.
proposals.method: We will extend a coordinate network architecture (e.g., an MLP mapping 3D coordinates to density and color) with a depth prediction head. A convolutional discriminator network will be trained adversarially against this generator, using a combination of a standard photometric loss, a scene flow consistency loss (inspired by 'Fast Neural Scene Flow'), and the adversarial GAN loss. The adversarial loss will be applied specifically to the rendered depth maps, encouraging them to lie on the manifold of real depth distributions from the training dataset (e.g., ScanNet or KITTI). We will investigate stabilizing techniques like Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty or LSGAN.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel adversarial training paradigm specifically tailored for geometric realism in neural implicit representations. It will deliver a method to significantly improve the perceptual quality and physical plausibility of monocular depth and 3D scene reconstruction from images. The proposed 'adversarial depth prior' could be integrated into various NeRF and neural scene flow pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While GANs are common for image synthesis, applying them directly as a prior on the *geometry* (depth maps) of a coordinate-based neural scene representation is novel. This differs from prior NeRF GANs that generate novel views, focusing instead on constraining the underlying 3D geometry to be realistic, which is a less explored and non-trivial challenge.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Scene Flow Priors from Web-Scale Video via Coordinate Network Distillation
proposals.motivation: Current neural scene flow and dynamic NeRF methods are limited by a lack of large-scale, diverse 3D motion data for training. This restricts their generalization and robustness in the wild. The vast quantity of unstructured monocular videos on the web presents an unprecedented opportunity to learn universal priors for scene dynamics, but leveraging this noisy, unlabeled data remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a coordinate-based neural network, trained at scale on web videos using a curriculum of self-supervised flow and photometric consistency losses, can distill a foundational prior for scene flow that significantly improves the performance and data-efficiency of downstream dynamic 3D vision tasks.
proposals.method: We will scale up training using a massive dataset of in-the-wild monocular web videos. Building on Dr. Lucey's expertise, we will employ a coordinate network architecture (e.g., an MLP mapping (x, y, t) to flow) due to its favorable memory footprint for large-scale training. The core training will use a multi-task objective combining: 1) a robust photometric reconstruction loss from 'Fast Neural Scene Flow', 2) a temporal consistency loss inspired by 'Flow Supervision for Deformable NeRF', and 3) a curvature-aware regularization from 'Curvature-Aware Training' to ensure smooth and physically plausible flow fields. We will implement a progressive curriculum, starting with simpler, filtered video data before introducing full web-scale complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale pre-trained model for neural scene flow, capable of being fine-tuned with minimal data for tasks like dynamic novel view synthesis, 4D reconstruction, and action recognition. It will establish a methodology for distilling 3D understanding from uncontrolled 2D video collections at scale, moving beyond curated datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses flow for supervision on single scenes or small datasets, the novelty lies in training a single coordinate network on orders-of-magnitude more diverse video to learn a generalizable, transferable prior for 3D motion, effectively creating a 'foundation model' for scene dynamics.

proposals.title: SparseVolTrack: Boundary-Aware 3D Correlation Learning from Non-Uniform Samples for Volumetric Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Analyzing dynamic 3D data, such as from low-frame-rate video or sparsely acquired medical scans, is hampered by irregular sampling and anisotropic resolution. Traditional dense volumetric methods fail under these constraints, while existing neural scene flow or NeRF techniques often require dense supervision or uniform data. A method that can learn robust 3D representations and track boundaries directly from sparse, non-uniform samples is critically needed for practical applications in resource-constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A 3D correlation filter can be effectively trained using a sparse, strategically sampled set of patches to learn boundary-aware features, enabling accurate volumetric tracking and segmentation without requiring dense volume supervision.
proposals.method: We propose a novel neural architecture integrating a sparse, learnable sampling module with a 3D continuous convolution-based correlation filter. Inspired by coordinate network parameterizations, the model will map 3D coordinates to feature representations. The sampling module, guided by a boundary saliency predictor, will select non-uniform patches focusing on structural edges and motion discontinuities. These sparse samples will train a volumetric correlation filter, regularized using curvature-aware principles to ensure smoothness in undersampled regions, enabling dense output prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new framework for volumetric analysis under sparse, non-uniform sampling, directly applicable to low-frame-rate video scene flow and anisotropic medical image segmentation. It will demonstrate how correlation filters and neural coordinate networks can be fused for efficient, boundary-precise 3D learning, reducing data acquisition requirements.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of sparse, non-uniform sampling with 3D correlation filter learning is novel, as is the direct training of such a filter for boundary-aware tasks using only a sparse subset of a volume, bypassing the need for dense volumetric supervision common in pre-2026 NeRF and scene flow works.

proposals.title: Apodized Convolutional Networks: Spectral-Spatial Regularization via Learned Windowing
proposals.motivation: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are powerful but their learned filters often exhibit sharp spatial discontinuities and high-frequency spectral artifacts, which can lead to instability in tasks sensitive to frequency content like image compression and texture synthesis. Pre-2026, methods for filter regularization (e.g., weight decay, spectral norm) do not explicitly enforce desirable spectral properties. Integrating signal processing principles of apodization into deep learning offers a principled way to learn smoother, more interpretable filters with controlled frequency response.
proposals.hypothesis: By parameterizing convolutional kernels as the product of a learnable base filter and a learned apodization window (e.g., parameterized Blackman), we can jointly optimize networks for task performance and for filters with reduced spectral leakage, leading to more stable and efficient feature representations.
proposals.method: We will design an Apodized Convolution layer where each filter is defined as W_apodized = W_base ⊙ A(θ). Here, W_base is a standard learnable kernel, and A(θ) is a spatially tapered window (e.g., generalized cosine window) with parameters θ learned via backpropagation. The layer will be integrated into a multi-scale CNN architecture. Training will use a combined loss: a standard task loss (e.g., reconstruction for denoising) and a novel spectral regularization term penalizing high-frequency energy in the filter's Fourier transform, computed efficiently via FFT. We will demonstrate performance on neural scene flow estimation and coordinate-based neural field tasks, where spectral artifacts can degrade results.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, interpretable method for spectral-spatial regularization within CNNs, directly bridging classical signal processing and deep learning. It will provide empirical evidence that apodized filters improve training stability and performance on frequency-sensitive vision tasks, and deliver a practical, plug-in layer for filter design.
proposals.novelty: While apodization is classic in signal processing, its integration as a learnable component within a CNN's filter parameterization is novel. This moves beyond simple post-hoc filtering or fixed windows, enabling the network to co-adapt window shape and filter features for the end task.

proposals.title: Deep Feature-Driven Filter Learning for Efficient Neural Scene Flow and View Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Recent work on efficient filter learning for coordinate-based networks (e.g., in NeRF and scene flow) has shown promise for fast training and inference. However, these methods often operate directly on raw coordinates or low-level scene encodings, lacking the robust, semantic-aware priors learned by deep convolutional networks. Integrating these deep features could significantly improve generalization, especially for dynamic or deformable scenes where data is sparse, a key challenge in pre-2026 neural rendering and 3D vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conditioning an efficient filter-learning framework (like a hash grid or factorized feature tensor) on multi-scale deep convolutional features extracted from input images will yield more accurate, robust, and faster-converging representations for tasks like neural scene flow and novel view synthesis, particularly under motion and deformation.
proposals.method: We will extend a state-of-the-art efficient coordinate network (e.g., an InstantNGP-style architecture) by integrating a parallel, frozen or lightly fine-tuned, pre-trained CNN backbone (e.g., a ResNet or Vision Transformer). The deep features, extracted at multiple scales, will be fused via cross-attention or concatenation with the coordinate-based filter features before the final MLP layers. For dynamic scenes, we will adapt the flow supervision and curvature-aware training techniques from Lucey's prior work to regularize the feature-conditioned filter field, ensuring temporal coherence and geometric plausibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new hybrid architecture that marries the efficiency of coordinate network filters with the strong inductive biases of deep features. We expect to demonstrate superior performance on benchmarks for neural scene flow (e.g., FlyingThings3D) and deformable NeRF, with faster convergence and improved robustness to occlusions and sparse views. The code and models will be released to advance research in efficient neural 3D reconstruction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses CNN features for initialization or in separate pipelines, the novelty lies in the direct, persistent conditioning of a real-time filter-learning framework on deep features throughout the optimization process, creating a synergistic model that is both efficient and semantically aware for dynamic 3D tasks.

proposals.title: Neural Scene Flow Fields with Explicit Non-Rigid Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Current neural scene flow methods, including Fast Neural Scene Flow, often assume a globally rigid transformation or treat motion as a generic displacement field, which is insufficient for complex real-world scenes containing both rigid body motion and articulated/non-rigid deformations. This limits their physical plausibility and accuracy in dynamic 3D reconstruction and understanding. A method that explicitly disentangles these motion types would provide a more accurate and interpretable representation of dynamic scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and decomposing scene flow into a global rigid component and a local non-rigid residual within a coordinate network framework will yield more accurate, physically plausible, and generalizable flow estimates than treating flow as a monolithic vector field.
proposals.method: We will extend coordinate-based neural scene flow networks by designing a two-branch architecture. One branch will regress a 6-DoF rigid transformation (using a SE(3) representation) for the entire scene or large segments. The second branch, a coordinate MLP, will estimate a corrective, non-rigid deformation field. The network will be trained with a composite loss combining flow supervision from datasets like FlyingThings3D, a rigidity-aware smoothness loss inspired by Curvature-Aware Training principles to regularize the non-rigid field, and a cycle-consistency constraint to enforce temporal coherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neural scene flow model that produces an explicit decomposition of motion, enhancing interpretability. It will advance the state-of-the-art in flow accuracy for mixed-motion scenes and provide a stronger foundation for downstream tasks like 4D dynamic scene reconstruction and robotic perception.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, learned decomposition within a single end-to-end coordinate network, moving beyond the monolithic flow estimation or post-hoc rigid fitting common in pre-2026 work, and integrating rigidity constraints directly into the training objective.

proposals.title: Category-Agnostic Neural Scene Flow via Meta-Learning and Geometric Priors
proposals.motivation: Current neural scene flow methods, including Fast Neural Scene Flow, achieve high performance but are typically trained and evaluated on datasets with limited object categories, leading to poor generalization to unseen shapes. This limits their real-world applicability in robotics and AR/VR, where systems must handle a vast, open-world variety of objects. A robust, category-agnostic scene flow estimator is a critical unsolved challenge in dynamic 3D vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural scene flow network can be trained to generalize to unseen object categories by meta-learning a shape-agnostic flow representation, regularized by explicit geometric constraints derived from coordinate network curvature, leading to robust performance without category-specific training data.
proposals.method: We will extend a coordinate-based neural scene flow network (like Fast Neural Scene Flow) with a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, where each training episode samples a different object category. The inner-loop adaptation will be regularized using a curvature-aware loss, inspired by Lucey's prior work, to enforce physically plausible deformations. We will integrate a flow supervision mechanism from deformable NeRF to provide additional temporal consistency signals, and evaluate generalization on held-out categories from datasets like ShapeNet and real-world scans.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first meta-learned, category-agnostic neural scene flow model, significantly advancing the robustness of dynamic 3D reconstruction. It will provide a new benchmark for generalization in scene flow and a publicly released framework for evaluating cross-category performance. The integration of meta-learning with explicit geometric priors offers a novel paradigm for improving neural network generalization in 3D vision tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of meta-learning for category generalization with explicit, curvature-based geometric regularization for scene flow—a fusion not explored in prior work. This moves beyond simply scaling training data or using broader datasets, instead learning a prior for how any rigid or deformable shape should move.

proposals.title: IR-NeRF: Iterative Refinement for Robust Neural Scene Flow and Pose Estimation under Large Misalignments
proposals.motivation: Neural scene flow and novel view synthesis methods, such as Deformable NeRF, are highly sensitive to accurate camera pose initialization. In real-world scenarios like robotics or dynamic SLAM, large initial pose misalignments are common, causing these networks to fail or converge to poor local minima. Current methods lack a robust, integrated mechanism to recover from such large errors, limiting their practical applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learnable, curvature-aware coordinate network with an iterative, flow-supervised pose refinement module will enable robust joint estimation of scene flow and camera pose, even under large initial misalignments, by leveraging the network's implicit scene representation for gradient-based optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture. First, a curvature-aware coordinate network will encode a canonical scene and a deformation field. Second, we will incorporate a differentiable iterative pose refinement (IPR) module, initialized from the large misalignment. This IPR module will use a flow supervision loss, computed between observed and network-rendered optical flow, to generate pose updates. The entire system will be trained end-to-end, allowing the scene representation and the refinement process to co-adapt, using a combined loss of photometric error, flow consistency, and a curvature regularization prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework that significantly improves the robustness of neural scene representation and flow estimation in the presence of severe pose errors. It will demonstrate how iterative refinement can be seamlessly integrated into a neural network training loop for joint optimization, advancing the state-of-the-art for dynamic 3D reconstruction in challenging, real-world conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses flow for supervision or refines poses, the novelty lies in explicitly designing the network and training to handle and correct *large* initial misalignments from the start, through a dedicated, co-trained refinement loop that leverages the implicit model's gradients, moving beyond small-error fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Registration Networks for Robust Non-Rigid Scene Flow and 4D Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Dynamic, non-rigid scene reconstruction from monocular or multi-view video is a core challenge in computer vision, with applications in robotics, AR/VR, and content creation. Existing Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) methods for dynamic scenes often struggle with fast motion, occlusions, or lack robust geometric priors, leading to artifacts and poor generalization. A method that integrates explicit geometric registration with implicit neural scene representations could significantly improve tracking fidelity and reconstruction quality in complex, real-world environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural registration network, trained with a combination of flow supervision and curvature-aware regularization, can provide temporally consistent and geometrically plausible correspondences that will serve as a powerful inductive bias for a dynamic NeRF, enabling high-fidelity 4D reconstruction of challenging non-rigid scenes without requiring pre-computed optical flow or 3D supervision.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture. A coordinate-based registration network, inspired by Lucey's prior work, will predict dense scene flow between frames, supervised by a photometric loss and a smoothness term regularized by a curvature-aware loss on the predicted flow field to prevent overfitting and ensure physical plausibility. This predicted flow will then warp a canonical NeRF representation, similar to Deformable NeRF, but crucially, the registration network's outputs will also provide a direct correspondence loss between time steps to constrain the deformation field, enforcing multi-view consistency and temporal coherence during the joint optimization of both networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for joint non-rigid tracking and 4D reconstruction that is more robust to occlusions and fast motion than flow-prior methods. It will demonstrate how geometric registration networks can be effectively integrated as a learned component within dynamic neural scene representations, advancing the state-of-the-art in unsupervised dynamic scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, bi-directional coupling of a task-specific neural registration network (with curvature-aware training) directly into the optimization loop of a dynamic NeRF, using its outputs not just as initialization but as a continuous, learnable correspondence constraint that improves both the flow and the reconstruction, a synergy not explored in prior work.

proposals.title: GraphFlowNet: Structure-Aware Scene Flow from Point Clouds via Dynamic Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Accurate, real-time scene flow estimation from point clouds is critical for dynamic 3D scene understanding in robotics and AR/VR. Existing neural methods, including coordinate networks and NeRF-based approaches, often struggle to efficiently capture the complex, non-rigid local motion and geometric relationships inherent in unstructured point cloud sequences. A method that explicitly models these local structures could significantly improve flow accuracy and robustness without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling point cloud sequences as spatio-temporal graphs and processing them with a dynamic graph neural network (GNN) will yield more accurate and generalizable scene flow estimates by explicitly learning from local geometric neighborhoods and their temporal evolution.
proposals.method: We will develop GraphFlowNet, a novel architecture that constructs a k-nearest neighbor graph over each point cloud frame. A hierarchical GNN (e.g., EdgeConv or Graph Attention Network) will extract local structural features. These features will be input to a recurrent or transformer-based temporal module to model motion across frames. The network will be trained end-to-end using a hybrid loss combining flow supervision (as in Fast Neural Scene Flow) with a novel curvature-aware regularization term (inspired by Curvature-Aware Training for Coordinate Networks) applied to the learned graph embeddings to encourage smooth and physically plausible motion fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new GNN-based architecture for scene flow that explicitly leverages point cloud topology, 2) a demonstration that geometric regularization at the graph level improves flow consistency, and 3) a performance benchmark showing superior accuracy and efficiency compared to point-based MLP or coordinate network methods on standard datasets like FlyingThings3D.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for static point cloud processing, their application to the temporal scene flow problem is novel. The key innovation is the integration of dynamic graph construction with curvature-aware regularization within a flow estimation framework, creating a structure-aware alternative to purely point-wise or implicit coordinate-based methods.

proposals.title: Meta-RegNet: Few-Shot Category-Agnostic Scene Flow via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Current neural scene flow and registration methods, including coordinate network-based approaches, require extensive per-category or per-scene training data, limiting their applicability to novel objects or environments. A model capable of rapid adaptation to new object categories from only a few examples would significantly enhance the utility of neural registration for robotics and AR/VR, where prior 3D models are often unavailable. Few-shot learning for 3D dynamic scene understanding remains a critical, unsolved challenge in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural scene flow network, meta-trained across a diverse set of object categories, can learn initialization parameters and adaptation strategies that enable accurate, category-agnostic registration on novel objects after only a few gradient steps using limited support examples.
proposals.method: We will build upon a coordinate-based neural scene flow network (like Fast Neural Scene Flow) as the base architecture. We will employ a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, where each 'task' corresponds to a distinct object category from a dataset like DeformingThings4D or NR4D. The inner-loop optimization will adapt the network's parameters using a small support set of scene flow examples from a novel category. The outer-loop meta-optimization will update the network's initial parameters to facilitate rapid inner-loop adaptation. We will integrate a curvature-aware training regularizer to ensure stable meta-learning on the coordinate network's implicit surface.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for few-shot, category-agnostic 3D scene registration, directly addressing a key bottleneck in real-world deployment. It will demonstrate how meta-learning principles can be effectively combined with coordinate network architectures for dynamic 3D tasks. The resulting model will set a new benchmark for data-efficient neural scene flow.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for image classification, its application to coordinate network-based 3D scene flow for few-shot registration is novel. The key innovation is the fusion of MAML with a curvature-regularized neural scene flow model to achieve fast adaptation on geometric registration tasks, moving beyond static shape reconstruction.

ideas.title: Invert the need for many views
ideas.description: Achieve joint pose optimization and scene reconstruction from very few input images.

ideas.title: Non-linear manifold sampling for structured bias generation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes learning a joint non-linear manifold that captures the intrinsic structure of both object shapes and the systematic biases (e.g., sensor artifacts, annotation errors, or demographic imbalances) present in real-world data. Instead of applying generic noise, the model inverts the typical correction objective to strategically sample from this manifold, generating novel but plausible shape-bias pairs for data augmentation. This creates a self-supervised training loop where a model is challenged with increasingly complex, structured adversarial examples it helped generate, leading to superior robustness and generalization. The core novelty is using the manifold's geometry to ensure generated biases are semantically meaningful and physically coherent with the underlying shape variation.

ideas.title: Robust Non-Linear Facial Shape Modeling via Joint Subspace Learning and Extreme Pose Synthesis
ideas.description: This research proposes a unified framework that tackles the dual challenge of modeling extreme facial articulations and learning from imperfect data. The core idea is to jointly learn a low-dimensional shape subspace while iteratively synthesizing plausible, extreme pose and expression data to augment the training set. The synthesized data, governed by the evolving subspace constraints, provides a strong regularization signal that helps disentangle true shape variation from annotation noise in the real training trajectories. This creates a virtuous cycle where a more robust subspace enables better synthesis of challenging cases, which in turn refines the subspace, leading to a model that generalizes reliably to both common and extreme facial configurations seen in the wild.

ideas.title: Robust Non-Linear Shape Inference with Unreliable Anchors
ideas.description: This research develops a method for inferring complete shapes from a set of potentially unreliable anchor landmark positions by leveraging a learned non-linear shape manifold. Instead of treating anchors as fixed constraints within a linear subspace, the approach jointly models the complex, continuous space of plausible shapes and an explicit 'reliability' or 'correction' model for the input anchors. This allows the system to denoise erroneous anchors, detect outliers, and infer the most probable true shape on the manifold even when a significant portion of the anchor data is corrupted or missing. The core synergy is that the rich, data-driven manifold provides the necessary prior to regularize the ill-posed problem of correcting the anchors, while the explicit handling of anchor unreality makes the non-linear model practical for real-world, noisy data.

ideas.title: Incorporate Learned Material Appearance Priors via Neural BRDFs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Physically Based Rendering, where complex material appearance is modeled using neural networks that learn a latent space for Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Functions (BRDFs). The analogous solution involves training compact neural networks on measured material data to create a continuous, low-dimensional prior for realistic appearance synthesis. Adapted to the subspace-constrained mean-shift context, we propose to train a similar neural appearance model (e.g., a small MLP or autoencoder) on a dataset of texture or appearance patches relevant to the target domain. This model learns a latent space of plausible appearances. During the mean-shift optimization, this network is integrated as a differentiable constraint, adding a loss term that penalizes deviations from the learned manifold of realistic textures. This guides the optimization not just by geometric subspace constraints, but by powerful, data-driven appearance priors, leading to more accurate and visually coherent results.

ideas.title: Model the Noise Manifold: Conditionally Generate Plausible Annotation Variants
ideas.description: This approach learns to generate the full distribution of possible noisy landmarks for a given clean input, conditioned on the source of noise. By using a conditional generative model, it captures how different annotators, ambiguous image features, or challenging conditions systematically bias annotations. This produces a more realistic and diverse set of training samples for any downstream inverse model. Consequently, a denoising or landmark detection model trained with this data becomes robust to the true variety of noise encountered in practice.

ideas.title: Physics/Task-Regularized Non-Linear Shape Manifold Learning
ideas.description: This approach learns a non-linear shape manifold where the representation is regularized by external, domain-specific knowledge to ensure plausibility and utility. Instead of relying solely on statistical data fitting, the manifold is constructed by an objective that combines data reconstruction loss with a physics-based simulation loss or a task-performance reward. This forces the latent space to organize shapes according to both perceptual similarity and functional or physical validity, dramatically improving generalization. The result is a compact, generative shape model that inherently avoids nonsensical interpolations and is robust to sparse or noisy training examples.

ideas.title: Robust Appearance-Constrained Shape Recovery via Joint Low-Rank and Sparse Decomposition
ideas.description: This method integrates Robust PCA for shape outlier rejection directly with the learning of a sophisticated appearance model within a unified optimization framework. Instead of treating shape cleaning and appearance modeling as separate pre-processing steps, they are jointly optimized: the low-rank shape subspace constrains the plausible deformations for appearance feature extraction, while the robust appearance model provides stronger correspondence cues to guide the separation of sparse landmark outliers from consistent shape. This synergy allows the system to bootstrap a high-fidelity model from imperfect, noisy initializations, where each component reinforces the other's robustness. The final output is a purified, jointly constrained model suitable for highly accurate fitting in challenging, uncontrolled conditions.

ideas.title: Single-Image Alignment via Bayesian Prior Smoothing
ideas.description: The original idea proposes scaling down a group-based alignment constraint to refine alignment for a single image using a prior. Drawing an analogy from geostatistics, specifically the Kriging method for spatial interpolation, which uses a prior covariance model (variogram) to estimate values at single points from sparse data. The analogous solution involves constructing a prior model of spatial correlation to guide interpolation. Adapted to the research context, this suggests constructing a learned prior model over the semantic or feature space of images (e.g., from a foundation model). For a single image, this prior defines a probabilistic neighborhood. The alignment process is then refined by imposing a constraint that the image's representation should be a probabilistically weighted interpolation (a 'smooth') of the representations within this neighborhood, as dictated by the prior, enabling precise single-image alignment without a group.

ideas.title: Amortized Variational Inference with Geometric Refinement for Robust Landmark Estimation
ideas.description: This approach replaces the standard Expectation-Maximization algorithm with a variational inference framework to model uncertainty in landmark positions more robustly. An amortized neural network proposes an initial distribution over landmarks conditioned on the input data, providing a stable starting point that mitigates poor local maxima. The system then employs a deterministic, differentiable geometric consistency module to refine these proposals, ensuring they obey spatial constraints. The combined training objective maximizes a variational lower bound regularized by geometric plausibility, leading to more stable convergence and improved accuracy.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Hypothesis Probabilistic Bundle Adjustment for Ambiguous Monocular Reconstruction
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the brittleness of classic bundle adjustment by embedding it within a robust, multi-hypothesis probabilistic framework. It maintains a population of shape parameter and landmark states, each associated with a likelihood that accounts for non-Gaussian noise and outlier processes through a mixture model. The optimization jointly refines these hypotheses and their weights using an EM-like algorithm that marginalizes over discrete latent variables for data association and model ambiguity. The output is a diverse set of plausible reconstructions with associated probabilities, providing robustness to outliers, dynamic elements, and the inherent ambiguities of monocular shape-from-template problems.

ideas.title: Apply semantic segmentation priors

ideas.title: Sparse, Self-Anchoring Representation Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that jointly learns to identify the most informative anchor points in a feature space while being trained only on a sparse, irregular set of data patches. The method uses the uncertainty and distribution of the sparse samples to guide the automatic selection of reliable anchors, and conversely, uses the evolving anchors to intelligently weight or hallucinate the influence of the sparse data. This creates a self-reinforcing system that achieves high robustness and representation power from limited, non-uniform training data, without manual annotation for landmarks or dense sampling grids. The core innovation is the closed-loop interaction between anchor optimization and sparse data assimilation.

ideas.title: Robust PCA-Guided Adversarial Noise Synthesis for Outlier-Aware Data Augmentation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using Robust PCA to decompose a dataset of noisy observations (e.g., landmark detections) into a low-rank consistent shape and a sparse outlier matrix. Instead of discarding the outlier component, its statistical and structural properties are analyzed and used to train a conditional generative model (e.g., a GAN or VAE) to synthesize novel, realistic outlier noise patterns. This synthesized outlier noise is then strategically injected into clean training data as an adversarial augmentation technique. The result is a self-improving pipeline that learns the 'failure modes' of a sensing system and uses that knowledge to generate challenging training examples, ultimately producing models with superior robustness to real-world corruption.

ideas.title: Apodized Deep Feature Learning for Spectral-Spatial Filter Regularization
ideas.description: This research direction proposes integrating apodization functions (e.g., Hamming, Blackman) directly into the filter learning process of a convolutional neural network. Instead of learning standard convolutional kernels, the method learns parameters that define an apodization window, which is then applied to a base filter derived from or regularized by deep feature activations from a preceding layer. This results in filters that are inherently smooth and tapered at their spatial boundaries, reducing spectral leakage and artifacts when processing feature maps as 2D signals. The synergy enables the joint optimization of filter shape for both task performance and desirable spectral properties, leading to more interpretable, stable, and efficient feature representations, particularly for tasks sensitive to frequency content like compression, denoising, or texture analysis.

ideas.title: Sparse Volumetric Correlation Learning for Boundary-Aware Tracking and Segmentation
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of sparse, non-uniform sampling with 3D correlation filters to create a novel framework for volumetric data. The core innovation is a boundary-aware 3D correlation filter that can be trained effectively using only a sparse, non-uniform set of patches sampled from a video volume or 3D medical scan, rather than requiring a dense sampling of the entire volume. This synergy directly addresses critical challenges in dynamic 3D data, such as irregular temporal sampling in video or anisotropic voxel spacing in medical imaging, where traditional dense sampling is infeasible. The resulting method enables accurate tracking and segmentation of 3D structures under realistic, resource-constrained acquisition scenarios, opening new applications in low-frame-rate video analysis and sparse-scan medical diagnostics.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Hierarchical Appearance Learning for Robust Subspace-Constrained Optimization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach integrates a hierarchical appearance representation into the subspace-constrained mean-shift framework to overcome brittleness. It uses a cascade of models, starting with illumination-invariant features for global guidance and progressively incorporating an online-adaptive texture model that updates from the current image sequence. Each component is associated with an uncertainty estimate, allowing the optimization to dynamically weight the influence of appearance constraints based on their predicted reliability, ensuring robustness to unseen conditions like lighting changes or occlusions.

ideas.title: Volumetric Spatio-Temporal Bundle Filtering for Coherent 4D Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research fuses boundary-aware 3D correlation filters with bundle adjustment principles to create a unified framework for reconstructing and tracking deforming volumetric structures, such as beating hearts in 4D CT or evolving neural vasculature in light-sheet microscopy. The method uses the 3D correlation filter to provide robust, locally consistent matches and initial shape estimates across a temporal volume. These estimates are then fed into a volumetric bundle adjustment system, which jointly refines the 3D positions of all landmark voxels or mesh vertices and their temporal trajectories, enforcing global consistency across the entire spatio-temporal sequence. The synergy produces a coherent 4D model that is simultaneously accurate in local matching and globally optimal in its temporal evolution, overcoming the drift and fragmentation typical of frame-to-frame 3D tracking.

ideas.title: Structure-Aware Robust PCA with Learned Outlier Models for Landmark Denoising
ideas.description: This approach enhances Robust PCA by explicitly modeling the structure of outliers, which are often correlated in time or space in landmark data. A neural module learns to predict likely outlier patterns from the input sequence, providing a structured prior that is integrated into the RPCA optimization. This prevents systematic errors, like tracking drift or partial occlusions, from being incorrectly absorbed into the recovered consistent shape. The system is trained end-to-end to decompose sequences into a clean low-rank shape and a structured outlier component, resulting in more robust outlier rejection.

ideas.title: Robust Subspace Identification via Iterative Refinement and Consensus
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'Dual' concept by drawing an analogy from exoplanet detection in astronomy, where a true low-dimensional periodic signal must be separated from high-dimensional systematic noise in light curves. The adapted solution is to develop an iterative refinement and consensus algorithm for 3D shape analysis. The method jointly learns the shape subspace (the underlying low-dimensional structure) while denoising the landmark trajectories (the high-dimensional noisy observations). Each iteration alternates between: 1) estimating the subspace from the current, possibly noisy, trajectory projections using a robust dimensionality reduction technique, and 2) denoising each individual trajectory by leveraging the consensus of the subspace model to identify and correct misalignments. This creates a virtuous cycle, progressively improving both the subspace model and the alignment quality until convergence.

ideas.title: Adaptively Gated Fusion of Deep Feature Priors for Robust and Efficient Filter Learning
ideas.description: This system overcomes the brittleness of naive integration by designing a meta-fusion framework for filter learning. A small conditioning network analyzes the target data and generates adaptive gating masks that modulate the influence of pre-trained deep convolutional features on the filter parameter updates. This allows the efficient filter learner to retain its core properties of speed and adaptability, using the deep features as a flexible, on-demand prior rather than a rigid constraint. The result is a robust model that maintains efficiency, avoids catastrophic interference from irrelevant pre-trained knowledge, and generalizes effectively across diverse tasks.

ideas.title: Leveraging Topological Data Analysis for Robust Pose and Expression Modeling
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to improve robustness to large out-of-plane rotations and strong facial deformations by drawing an analogy from computational biology. In protein structure prediction, Topological Data Analysis (TDA) is used to model highly flexible molecular shapes by extracting topological features (like loops and voids) that are invariant to continuous deformations. We adapt this solution by developing a method to convert facial data (3D mesh or 2D landmarks) into a topological signature, such as a persistence image, that captures the essential facial structure (e.g., the loops of the mouth and eye sockets) across scales. This deformation- and rotation-invariant representation would then be used as a robust feature for facial analysis tasks, making models significantly more resilient to extreme poses and expressions without requiring perfect geometric normalization.

ideas.title: Ensemble-Guided Single-Image Alignment via Adaptive Prior Fusion
ideas.description: This approach refines alignment for a single image by leveraging not one but a dynamically assembled set of complementary priors. A small conditioning network analyzes the input image to select and fuse alignment signals from this ensemble, which may include textual descriptions, concept embeddings, and stylistic references. This creates a robust, multi-faceted constraint that mitigates the brittleness of single-prior methods. The system thus achieves precise, context-sensitive alignment that is resilient to poor prior selection or adversarial perturbations.

ideas.title: Dense Patch Exploitation with Graph-Based Identity Reasoning for Multi-Object Tracking
ideas.description: This system extends dense patch exploitation by coupling it with a dedicated identity reasoning graph to solve multi-object tracking. The dense patch backbone provides rich, localized appearance and motion features for all potential objects. These features are then used to construct and continuously update a spatio-temporal graph that explicitly models hypotheses for object identities, their interactions, and occlusion events. This joint framework allows the system to maintain consistent tracks through complex scenarios by resolving ambiguities that pure patch similarity would fail on, resulting in more robust multi-object tracking performance.

ideas.title: Scale down for mobile deployment

ideas.title: Apodized Single-Image Alignment via Spectral Prior Tapering
ideas.description: This research direction adapts the mathematical framework of group constraints for single-image alignment, but critically modifies it by applying apodization functions (like Hann or Blackman-Harris windows) from spectroscopy. The apodization functions are used to taper the influence of the learned prior—such as a diffusion model prior or a learned manifold—as it is applied across the spatial or feature domains of the single image. This smooth tapering mitigates hard boundary artifacts and spectral distortions that typically arise when enforcing global constraints on a single, local data instance, leading to more stable and physically plausible alignments or reconstructions without requiring multiple views or a dataset.

ideas.title: Spatially-Varying Filtering for Single-Image Alignment with Non-Stationary Priors
ideas.description: This research direction develops a method for aligning a single, potentially degraded image to a prior template or model. It uniquely addresses the common scenario where the image's noise, blur, or background statistics are non-stationary, changing significantly across different regions (e.g., due to uneven illumination or mixed scene content). The core innovation is to jointly estimate the alignment transformation and a spatially-adaptive filter model, where the filter's local parameters are regularized by the group constraint of the alignment task. This creates a tight feedback loop: the alignment informs where the background is non-stationary, and the locally-adapted filtering cleans the signal to achieve a more stable and accurate alignment than possible with a global stationary assumption or without the alignment prior.

ideas.title: Apply semantic segmentation to map-constrained regions
ideas.description: Use driveable area segmentation from maps as a strong prior for camera-based object detection.
ideas.sourceTitle: Argoverse: 3D Tracking and Forecasting With Rich Maps

ideas.title: Cross-Subject Manifold Alignment for Generalized Multi-Object Tracking
ideas.description: This research fuses manifold alignment for cross-subject transfer with dense patch-based multi-object tracking. The core idea is to learn a unified, low-dimensional manifold that aligns the visual and temporal feature dynamics of multiple objects (like limbs or tools) across different human subjects or agents. By aligning these manifolds, the dense patch exploitation framework can generalize its tracking and detection capabilities to a novel subject, overcoming individual variations in appearance or motion style. This results in a system capable of zero-shot or few-shot adaptation for multi-object tracking in personalized applications like sports analysis or rehabilitation, without needing extensive labeled data for each new person.

ideas.title: 3D Spatio-Temporal Graph Matching for Volumetric Anchor Consistency
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel framework for analyzing 3D volumetric data (e.g., video+time, CT/MRI scans) by fusing graph matching with boundary-aware correlation filters. The core innovation is to construct spatio-temporal graphs from candidate anchor points detected across the 3D volume and its temporal ensemble. Graph matching algorithms are then used to find the most globally consistent set of anchors, enforcing topological and geometric constraints. This consistent anchor graph subsequently guides and stabilizes a 3D extension of the correlation filter framework, leading to highly accurate, boundary-respecting tracking, segmentation, or registration that is robust to noise, occlusion, and complex deformations.

ideas.title: Integrate Deep Feature Priors into Efficient Filter Learning via a Hybrid Physics-Informed Architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), specifically hybrid RANS-LES modeling, to the problem of integrating deep features into efficient filter learning. In CFD, hybrid models efficiently combine a fast, approximate base solver (RANS) with targeted injections of high-fidelity simulation (LES) data where needed most. The analogous solution is adapted by framing the efficient filter learning algorithm as the fast base model and a pre-trained deep convolutional network as the source of high-fidelity feature priors. The core research challenge becomes designing a lightweight, data-dependent gating network that analyzes the input and decides when and where to fuse deep feature representations into the filter parameter updates or the filtering operation itself. This creates a hybrid, physics-informed (or prior-informed) architecture that maintains computational efficiency for simple signal components while leveraging deep representation power for complex structures, leading to filters that are both efficient and semantically aware.

ideas.title: Incremental Graph Matching for Long-Term Video Landmark Consistency
ideas.description: This research develops an online framework for aligning semantic landmarks across massive, continuous video datasets. It models the temporal correspondence problem as an incremental graph matching task, where new video frames are matched to a dynamically maintained 'consistency graph' of anchor points. This allows the system to enforce long-term global consistency across the entire stream, preventing the drift typical of purely local incremental methods. The result is a scalable system capable of aligning landmarks in hours-long or even days-long video with a stable, evolving semantic map.

ideas.title: Adopt probabilistic deep learning for uncertainty
ideas.description: Output a distribution over possible transformations to quantify registration uncertainty.

ideas.title: Topology-Aware Adaptive Alignment for Arbitrary Deformable Objects
ideas.description: This research introduces a meta-framework that first learns a category-specific topological model to understand part decomposition, adjacency, and articulation patterns for a new object class. This model then informs the dynamic generation of anchor points and adaptive sparsity groups, replacing the static groupings used for faces. The core alignment process is augmented with a gating network that selects from a suite of constraint strategies (including the grouped L1-norm) based on the inferred topology and observed occlusion, enabling robust application to highly variable categories like hands, animals, and clothed bodies.

ideas.title: Adapting Geodetic Survey Principles for Deformable Object Alignment
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geodesy. The analogous problem is establishing a stable reference frame on a deforming surface (the Earth's crust), solved by defining fixed control points. Adapting this, the research idea is to test the grouped L1-norm anchored alignment framework on non-face objects like hands or animals by first defining a category-specific 'stable core' of anatomical anchor points. The framework would be adapted to strongly weight the alignment of these anchors, creating a robust reference frame. This approach would then allow for the principled alignment and comparison of the more variable parts of the object's structure, effectively separating the problem of global pose estimation from that of measuring local non-rigid deformation.

ideas.title: Preconditioned Subspace Learning for Robust Filtering with Noisy Trajectories
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel framework that unifies subspace learning and efficient numerical solving for filtering problems with noisy data. The core idea is to formulate the joint problem of learning a shape subspace and denoising landmark trajectories as a large-scale, structured linear system that evolves during optimization. By designing specialized preconditioners derived from the current estimate of the low-dimensional subspace, the iterative solver for the filter's update equations is dramatically accelerated. This creates a virtuous cycle where a better subspace estimate yields a more effective preconditioner, which in turn speeds up the convergence of the joint denoising and subspace refinement, enabling the method to scale to problems with massive numbers of noisy trajectories that were previously intractable.

ideas.title: Structured Bias Manifold Learning for Controllable and Realistic Fairness Augmentation
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond generating simple 'biased noise' to instead learn a structured, disentangled latent manifold representing realistic bias features (e.g., spurious correlations, demographic biases). The system is trained adversarially on datasets known to contain specific biases, forcing it to capture complex bias patterns. It can then sample from or perturb this manifold to generate controlled, realistic bias transformations for data augmentation. The ultimate goal is to produce models that are more robust to a wide spectrum of real-world biases by having been exposed to and trained against their learned, structured simulations during training.

ideas.title: Use graph neural networks to model landmark relationships

ideas.title: Adapting EM for Latent Landmarks via Geophysical Signal Deconvolution
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Expectation-Maximization framework used for latent variable estimation in geophysical signal deconvolution to the problem of landmark localization. In seismic imaging, EM is used to separate the latent earth reflectivity from the known source wavelet and noise, iteratively refining both the latent signal and model parameters. Analogously, for landmark detection, the correct landmark positions are treated as latent variables, and the noisy, biased detections are modeled as the output of an observation process. The adapted EM algorithm will, in the E-step, estimate the posterior distribution of true landmark locations given the current observation model (e.g., a Gaussian noise model with bias), and in the M-step, update the parameters of that observation model (means and covariances of the noise/bias). This process iteratively 'deconvolves' the detection noise, converging on more accurate and consistent landmark positions while jointly learning the characteristics of the detection system's errors.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Horizon Tracking for 3D Boundary-Aware Correlation Filters
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the boundary-aware correlation filter framework to 3D volumetric data by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic interpretation. In that field, a key challenge is tracking geological horizons (boundaries) through 3D seismic volumes. The analogous solution uses 3D structure tensors to estimate the local orientation (dip and azimuth) of geological layers, which then guides subsequent processing like steering filters or anisotropic diffusion to enhance features along the layer while smoothing within it, thereby respecting the 3D boundary structure. Adapted to this research context, the method would involve computing a 3D structure tensor from the input volumetric data (e.g., CT scan, video cube) to estimate the local orientation of surfaces or boundaries. This tensor would then steer the learning and application of a 3D correlation filter, constraining it to be correlated along the detected surfaces and decorrelated across them. This creates a volumetric filter inherently aware of complex 3D boundaries, improving tracking and segmentation in medical imaging and video analysis by preventing filter corruption across distinct anatomical structures or moving objects.

ideas.title: Use a Diffusion Model Manifold for Shape Representation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that shape variability resides in a linear subspace (e.g., PCA). Drawing an analogy from molecular dynamics, where diffusion maps are used to model the non-linear manifold of protein folding conformations, we propose to replace the linear shape model with a manifold learned via diffusion maps. The analogous solution involves building a graph from sampled shapes, computing pairwise affinities using a shape descriptor, and performing spectral analysis to extract the dominant non-linear modes of variation. The adapted idea applies this to 3D shape representation: a diffusion operator is constructed from a dataset of shapes, and its eigenfunctions provide intrinsic, low-dimensional coordinates that parameterize the non-linear shape manifold, potentially capturing complex deformations and transitions more naturally than linear subspaces.

ideas.title: EM-Guided Non-Periodic Correlation for Robust Sub-Pixel Landmark Refinement
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework for landmark localization that fuses non-periodic Fourier correlation with expectation-maximization. The model treats the true sub-pixel landmark positions as latent variables, while simultaneously estimating the parameters of a correlation filter explicitly designed to avoid boundary artifacts. The E-step infers probable landmark locations given the current filter, and the M-step updates the filter to maximize response at these locations without assuming signal periodicity. This co-optimization yields a filter tailored to the image's actual content and boundaries, enabling highly accurate localization where standard periodic-correlation methods fail.

ideas.title: Dual-Memory Meta-Learner for Robust Long-Term Visual Tracking
ideas.description: This system robustly handles severe appearance changes by separating adaptation into two interacting components: a stable long-term memory that stores a protected, diverse set of target representations, and a rapidly adaptable short-term memory for immediate adjustments. A meta-learner module evaluates the reliability of short-term updates by checking their geometric and feature-space consistency with the long-term model and recent high-confidence predictions. Only updates that pass this verification are used to carefully augment the long-term memory, preventing catastrophic forgetting and model drift while maintaining the ability to adapt to genuine, persistent appearance changes.

ideas.title: Incremental Video Landmark Alignment via Seismic Signal Processing Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from geophysics, specifically seismic signal processing for continuous data streams, to the problem of aligning landmarks in massive video datasets. In seismology, techniques like incremental cross-correlation, dynamic time warping with sliding windows, and hierarchical constraint propagation allow real-time alignment of seismic phases across distributed sensor networks processing terabytes of streaming data. Adapted to video analysis, we develop an online/incremental algorithm that treats landmark trajectories as multi-dimensional time-series signals. The method processes video in sliding windows, performing incremental cross-correlation of landmark motion patterns and propagating alignment constraints hierarchically from well-correlated segments to ambiguous ones. This enables continuous, memory-efficient alignment for very long video streams without requiring full dataset storage or reprocessing, effectively scaling landmark alignment to massive video datasets through seismic-inspired streaming techniques.

ideas.title: Multi-Identity Ensemble Alignment with Spatio-Temporal Identity Association
ideas.description: This method generalizes ensemble alignment to multi-person videos by jointly optimizing for representation alignment and robust identity tracking. A graph-based association module explicitly models the temporal evolution and interactions between all detected subjects, preventing identity swaps. The framework enforces consistency both within each subject's trajectory and across the entire ensemble of identities, improving robustness to occlusion and appearance changes. The result is a unified system that produces aligned representations for multiple people while maintaining their distinct identities throughout a video sequence.

ideas.title: Multi-Subject Ensemble via Domain-Invariant Representation Learning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption in face recognition ensemble methods that all input images must depict the same person. Drawing an analogy from bioinformatics, specifically metagenomic analysis of mixed microbial communities, we propose a solution where the input image set is treated as a 'mixed sample'. Similar to how tools like SourceTracker deconvolve DNA sequences into constituent species using reference databases, we can develop a model that uses a large-scale face gallery as a reference. This model would learn domain-invariant, identity-specific features and employ statistical disentanglement or attention mechanisms to identify and weight the potential contributions of multiple candidate identities present within the input ensemble, thereby relaxing the single-subject constraint entirely.

ideas.title: Disentangled Identity-Preserving Multi-Subject Ensemble
ideas.description: This system overcomes the identity-blending flaw by enforcing a strict separation between subject identity and other visual attributes. It processes a primary image to define a target identity vector, which is held invariant. Auxiliary images from any number of different subjects are then analyzed to extract non-identity feature maps representing pose, illumination, background, or expression. A transformer-based fusion module, guided by the primary identity, integrates these auxiliary attributes to synthesize novel renditions of the primary subject. The result is a robust ensemble method that leverages multi-subject data without corrupting the core identity, enabling applications like pose transfer from one person to another or robust re-lighting.

ideas.title: Multi-Object Tracking via Dense Patch Graph Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea extends the dense patch exploitation framework to multi-object tracking and detection by drawing an analogy from computational biology. The analogous problem is identifying distinct protein complexes from a noisy, dense interaction network, which is solved using graph partitioning algorithms. The adapted solution constructs a spatio-temporal graph where nodes are dense patches and edges represent their feature affinities. By applying dynamic community detection to this graph, the framework can simultaneously segment the scene into multiple object clusters (detection) and maintain these cluster identities over time (tracking), effectively solving the multi-object problem through patch-level graph analysis.

ideas.title: Robust: Bootstrap and Iteratively Refine Subspace with Explicit Outlier Modeling
ideas.description: This method breaks the circular dependency between subspace learning and denoising by introducing a robust bootstrapping stage. It begins by applying conservative temporal or consensus-based filtering to obtain an initial stable subset of landmark trajectories. A low-rank subspace is learned from this subset, providing a prior to distinguish inliers from outliers in the full, noisy data. The system then alternates between refining the subspace using a weighted, robust objective that down-weights outliers and updating the denoised trajectories, progressively relaxing the conservatism of the initial filter as the model becomes more confident.

ideas.title: Adversarial Immunoassay for Boundary Artifact Detection
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, where assays are designed to detect pathogens by identifying their unique molecular markers, this idea inverts the standard filter learning objective for anomaly detection. The analogous solution is the development of highly specific antibodies that bind only to foreign epitopes. Adapted to machine learning, we propose training a filter (a 'convolutional antibody') with an adversarial objective: its parameters are learned to maximize activation on training samples containing known boundary artifacts (e.g., from data splicing, compression, or generative model failures) while simultaneously minimizing activation on pristine data. This creates a filter that is not a feature detector for normal content, but is instead maximally sensitive to the 'foreign' signal of boundary inconsistencies, turning it into a specialized anomaly detector for artifacts.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Approximate Graph Matching for Scalable Anchor Consistency
ideas.description: This system overcomes the computational intractability of exact graph matching for anchor consistency by employing a multi-stage, hierarchical approach. First, it uses efficient spectral embeddings or lightweight graph neural networks to generate coarse, consistent node correspondences across the ensemble, drastically reducing the problem size. Second, it applies a robust, graduated non-convex optimization only on this pruned set of candidate anchors, ensuring high-quality matches. This method provides a tunable trade-off between computational efficiency and matching accuracy, making it viable for large-scale, real-world ensemble data where the original idea would fail.
